Laksa with tau pok fishcake egg and clams

food_subgroup: Noodle soup dish with seafood and coconut-based broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general laksa entry (FOODON_03311069); no specific clam laksa variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific laksa code. A0CKP (Noodle soup) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for laksa or clam noodle soup dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of laksa with clams as served at Qi Ji, approximately 500 g, comprising noodles, clams, tau pok, fishcake, hard-boiled egg, and coconut-based broth.

nutrient_source.title: Laksa, with coconut milk — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists laksa (coconut milk-based) per 100 g. The Qi Ji variant includes clams as the primary protein alongside fishcake, tau pok, and hard-boiled egg. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB laksa (coconut milk) reference with minor upward adjustment to protein and sodium to reflect the clam and fishcake protein contribution, consistent with HPB seafood laksa data. Values represent a composite estimate for the full bowl including broth and toppings, expressed per 100 g of total dish weight.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, cooked — Glycemic Index Database; and coconut milk laksa analogue estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for clam laksa. GI estimated at 47 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (cooked, GI ~53) and the moderating effect of the high-fat coconut milk broth and protein-rich toppings (clams, fishcake, egg), which lower the effective glycemic response. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (47 × 45.5 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 21.4, rounded to 21.0. This is consistent with published analogous estimates for coconut milk-based noodle soups in the peer-reviewed literature.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from coconut broth, shrimp paste, and fishcake
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g bowl provides approximately 2100 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should consume this dish infrequently and avoid drinking the broth to reduce sodium intake substantially.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrates, partially offset by fat and protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of 21 per serving is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion size, request less noodles, and pair with additional protein to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from coconut milk and high sodium from broth and processed ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The coconut milk broth contributes significant saturated fat (~14 g per serving) and the dish is high in sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency, avoid the broth, and choose lower-saturated-fat alternatives where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Laksa with Clams is a Singaporean coconut milk-based noodle soup dish served at Qi Ji, a local hawker-style chain. The dish features thick rice vermicelli (mee hoon) submerged in a rich, aromatic coconut milk broth made from a spiced rempah paste incorporating dried shrimps, lemongrass, galangal, and chilli. Toppings include fresh clams, sliced fishcake, tau pok (fried tofu puffs), and a hard-boiled egg. The broth is creamy, mildly spicy, and fragrant with laksa leaves (daun kesum). Clams contribute a briny, oceanic sweetness that complements the richness of the coconut base. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~460 kcal per bowl), provides meaningful protein from clams, fishcake, and egg (~29 g per serving), and is relatively high in saturated fat from coconut milk (~14 g per serving) and sodium (~2100 mg per serving). The glycemic load is moderate due to the rice vermicelli carbohydrate content, though the fat and protein matrix moderates the glycemic response. Clinically, the dish is of concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat content. It is not suitable for individuals on strict low-sodium or low-saturated-fat diets without modification. The dish contains shellfish, crustaceans, egg, fish, and soy allergens and is not halal-certified at this outlet due to the presence of shellfish and the restaurant's mixed menu.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo laksa (coconut milk) reference data, which is an approved primary source for Singaporean dishes. The Qi Ji-specific clam variant is not individually listed in HPB Foodo; values have been adjusted to reflect the clam and fishcake protein contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using rice noodles as the closest anchor. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in coconut milk-based dishes per HPB data. Serving size of 500 g is an estimate for a standard hawker bowl based on HPB standard serving size guidance for noodle soup dishes. No customization of the Qi Ji item is permitted per the source menu description. The dish contains shrimps as declared by the restaurant. Halal status is not confirmed for this outlet. GI estimated at 47 (low) using rice noodles as the closest analogous food from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by coconut milk fat and protein content.
